During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. File:祐徳稲荷神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG|Haiden File:祐徳稲荷神社 命婦社.JPG|Myōbu-sha (Prefectural Cultural Property) File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩本社.JPG|Iwamoto-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 石壁社.JPG|Sekkizan-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩崎社.JPG|Iwasaki-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 奥の院.JPG|Oku-no-in File:祐徳稲荷神社 参集殿.JPG|Sanjusendo Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社外苑 祐徳博物館.JPG|Yūtoku Museum File:祐徳稲荷神社 錦破川.JPG|View from the Kinpaku River == Cultural Properties == === Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Myōbu-sha * Sword * Ancient documents == Access == From [[Saga Station]] or [[Hizen-Kashima Station]], take the [[Yūtoku Bus]] bound for Yūtoku Shrine (approximately 1 hour from Saga Station, 10 minutes from Hizen-Kashima Station). During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. File:Honden of Yutoku Inari Shrine.jpg File:Yutoku inari Shrine kagura-b.jpg File:「祐徳稲荷神社」楼門 - panoramio.jpg File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG == See also == *[[Fushimi Inari]] ==References== *[http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Official website] (in Japanese) {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yutoku Inari Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] [[Category:shinto shrines]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari Shrine}}[[category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{Inari Faith}}

File:祐徳稲荷神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG|Haiden File:祐徳稲荷神社 命婦社.JPG|Myōbu-sha (Prefectural Cultural Property) File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩本社.JPG|Iwamoto-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 石壁社.JPG|Sekkizan-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩崎社.JPG|Iwasaki-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 奥の院.JPG|Oku-no-in File:祐徳稲荷神社 参集殿.JPG|Sanjusendo Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社外苑 祐徳博物館.JPG|Yūtoku Museum File:祐徳稲荷神社 錦破川.JPG|View from the Kinpaku River == Cultural Properties == === Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Myōbu-sha * Sword * Ancient documents == Access == From [[Saga Station]] or [[Hizen-Kashima Station]], take the [[Yūtoku Bus]] bound for Yūtoku Shrine (approximately 1 hour from Saga Station, 10 minutes from Hizen-Kashima Station). During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]

Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]

Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. File:祐徳稲荷神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG|Haiden File:祐徳稲荷神社 命婦社.JPG|Myōbu-sha (Prefectural Cultural Property) File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩本社.JPG|Iwamoto-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 石壁社.JPG|Sekkizan-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩崎社.JPG|Iwasaki-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 奥の院.JPG|Oku-no-in File:祐徳稲荷神社 参集殿.JPG|Sanjusendo Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社外苑 祐徳博物館.JPG|Yūtoku Museum File:祐徳稲荷神社 錦破川.JPG|View from the Kinpaku River == Cultural Properties == === Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Myōbu-sha * Sword * Ancient documents == Access == From [[Saga Station]] or [[Hizen-Kashima Station]], take the [[Yūtoku Bus]] bound for Yūtoku Shrine (approximately 1 hour from Saga Station, 10 minutes from Hizen-Kashima Station). During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. File:Honden of Yutoku Inari Shrine.jpg File:Yutoku inari Shrine kagura-b.jpg File:「祐徳稲荷神社」楼門 - panoramio.jpg File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG == See also == *[[Fushimi Inari]] ==References== *[http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Official website] (in Japanese) {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yutoku Inari Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] [[Category:shinto shrines]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari Shrine}}[[category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{Inari Faith}}

File:祐徳稲荷神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG|Haiden File:祐徳稲荷神社 命婦社.JPG|Myōbu-sha (Prefectural Cultural Property) File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩本社.JPG|Iwamoto-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 石壁社.JPG|Sekkizan-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩崎社.JPG|Iwasaki-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 奥の院.JPG|Oku-no-in File:祐徳稲荷神社 参集殿.JPG|Sanjusendo Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社外苑 祐徳博物館.JPG|Yūtoku Museum File:祐徳稲荷神社 錦破川.JPG|View from the Kinpaku River == Cultural Properties == === Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Myōbu-sha * Sword * Ancient documents == Access == From [[Saga Station]] or [[Hizen-Kashima Station]], take the [[Yūtoku Bus]] bound for Yūtoku Shrine (approximately 1 hour from Saga Station, 10 minutes from Hizen-Kashima Station). During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. File:Honden of Yutoku Inari Shrine.jpg File:Yutoku inari Shrine kagura-b.jpg File:「祐徳稲荷神社」楼門 - panoramio.jpg File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG == See also == *[[Fushimi Inari]] ==References== *[http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Official website] (in Japanese) {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yutoku Inari Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] [[Category:shinto shrines]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari Shrine}}[[category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded_pages_from_Wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{Inari Faith}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. File:祐徳稲荷神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG|Haiden File:祐徳稲荷神社 命婦社.JPG|Myōbu-sha (Prefectural Cultural Property) File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩本社.JPG|Iwamoto-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 石壁社.JPG|Sekkizan-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩崎社.JPG|Iwasaki-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 奥の院.JPG|Oku-no-in File:祐徳稲荷神社 参集殿.JPG|Sanjusendo Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社外苑 祐徳博物館.JPG|Yūtoku Museum File:祐徳稲荷神社 錦破川.JPG|View from the Kinpaku River == Cultural Properties == === Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Myōbu-sha * Sword * Ancient documents == Access == From [[Saga Station]] or [[Hizen-Kashima Station]], take the [[Yūtoku Bus]] bound for Yūtoku Shrine (approximately 1 hour from Saga Station, 10 minutes from Hizen-Kashima Station). During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. File:Honden of Yutoku Inari Shrine.jpg File:Yutoku inari Shrine kagura-b.jpg File:「祐徳稲荷神社」楼門 - panoramio.jpg File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG == See also == *[[Fushimi Inari]] ==References== *[http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Official website] (in Japanese) {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yutoku Inari Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] [[Category:shinto shrines]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari Shrine}}[[category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{Inari Faith}}

During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles. File:祐徳稲荷神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG|Haiden File:祐徳稲荷神社 命婦社.JPG|Myōbu-sha (Prefectural Cultural Property) File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩本社.JPG|Iwamoto-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 石壁社.JPG|Sekkizan-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 岩崎社.JPG|Iwasaki-sha File:祐徳稲荷神社 奥の院.JPG|Oku-no-in File:祐徳稲荷神社 参集殿.JPG|Sanjusendo Hall File:祐徳稲荷神社外苑 祐徳博物館.JPG|Yūtoku Museum File:祐徳稲荷神社 錦破川.JPG|View from the Kinpaku River == Cultural Properties == === Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * Myōbu-sha * Sword * Ancient documents == Access == From [[Saga Station]] or [[Hizen-Kashima Station]], take the [[Yūtoku Bus]] bound for Yūtoku Shrine (approximately 1 hour from Saga Station, 10 minutes from Hizen-Kashima Station). During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. File:Honden of Yutoku Inari Shrine.jpg File:Yutoku inari Shrine kagura-b.jpg File:「祐徳稲荷神社」楼門 - panoramio.jpg File:祐徳稲荷神社 拝殿.JPG == See also == *[[Fushimi Inari]] ==References== *[http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Official website] (in Japanese) {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yutoku Inari Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] [[Category:shinto shrines]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari Shrine}}[[category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[en:Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] {{Inari Faith}}

During the New Year period, traffic congestion is common near Hyakkanbashi (on the border between [[Shiroishi, Saga|Shiroishi]] and Kashima) due to the influx of visitors. Until 1931, the Yūtoku Tramway extended to the shrine, but it was discontinued with the opening of the current [[Nagasaki Main Line]]. The bus service of Yūtoku Tramway evolved into the present-day [[Yūtoku Bus]]. == Baseball Stadium == The shrine once operated a baseball field called the "Yūtoku International Ground", built in 1935. This ground was located where the current visitor parking lot is situated. On April 21, 1950, a Central League official game was held here between the [[Nishinippon Pirates]] and the [[Chunichi Dragons]], where the Dragons won 12-1. In this game, Chunichi pitcher [[Shigeru Sugishita]] hit the first grand slam by a pitcher in Central League history. The ground was abandoned by 1957 due to fire and other factors. === Professional Baseball Official Game Records === Two official games were held here: one Central League game and one Pacific League game. * April 21, 1950: Nishinippon Pirates 1-12 Chunichi Dragons * April 1, 1952: Nishitetsu Lions 5-2 Hankyu Braves == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Tourist attractions in Saga Prefecture]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yutoku Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.yutokusan.jp/ Yūtoku Inari Shrine] - Official site * {{YouTube|iQs5rEB4Ruw|祐徳神社お火たき（鹿島市） 佐賀の音100選（重）}} - [[Saga Shimbun]] (published November 27, 2015) * [http://www.hizennya.com/fs/sake/c/yutoku Sake themed after Yūtoku Inari Shrine] {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yūtoku Inari}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:History of Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kashima, Saga]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated tangible cultural properties]] == History == Dedicated to [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the ''[[kami]]'' whose messengers are foxes, it is the third largest of its kind in Japan. It was constructed in 1688 as the family shrine of the [[Nabeshima clan]] who ruled what would become the Saga area (called [[Hizen Province|Hizen]] at that time) during the [[Edo period]]. It was built to the Inari ''kami'' of the harvest by a princess from [[Kyoto]] named Manko Hime (萬子媛) who married [[Nabeshima Naotomo]]. The Nabeshima clan was entrusted with protecting Edo’s interests in [[Kyūshū]], in particular the city of [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], which was one of the few ports open to foreign contact during Japan’s period of isolation. The shrine is also popular for new year celebrations and is very crowded during the first two weeks of January. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image = Yūtoku Inari Shrine sanmon and main hall.JPG | caption = Yūtoku Inari Shrine Entrance | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Yutoku Inari Shrine Autumn 2015.JPG|thumb|260px|right|Yutoku Inari Shrine in the Autumn of 2015]] {{nihongo|'''Yūtoku Inari Shrine'''|祐徳稲荷神社| Yūtoku Inari Jinja}} is a shrine located in [[Kashima, Saga|Kashima City]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is one of the most famous [[Inari Shrine|Inari shrines]] in Japan. ==Overview== {{Otheruses|祐徳稲荷神社 located in Kashima, Saga Prefecture|the shrine located in [[Togitsu, Nagasaki]]|Yūtoku Inari Shrine (Togitsu)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name= Yūtoku Inari Shrine | image=[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 楼門.JPG|250px|Rōmon]]
Rōmon
[[File:祐徳稲荷神社 main hall.JPG|250px|Honden]]
Haiden | location=[[Saga Prefecture]], [[Kashima]], Furue | coordinates = {{coord|33|4|25|N|130|06|30|E|region:JP-41|display=it}} | deity=[[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami]]
[[Ōmiyanome|Ōmiyanome-no-Ōkami]]
[[Sarutahiko|Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami]]
Shinreishimeime-no-Ōkami
Mankohime-no-Ōkami | type=Former [[Kensha|Prefectural Shrine]]
[[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] | established=[[Jōkyō]] 4 (1687) | alternative name=Chinzei Nikkō | festival=Early March | 神事=Ohitaki festival | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=170|marker=religious-shinto|type2=shape|fill2=#f5fffa|stroke-color2=#006400|stroke-width2=1|frame-latitude=33.086|frame-longitude=130.109}}{{location map|Japan Saga#Japan|float=center|width=200}} }} '''Yūtoku Inari Shrine''' (祐徳稲荷神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kashima]], [[Saga Prefecture]]. It is a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha|Bekkaku Shrine]] and its former rank was that of a prefectural shrine. It is also known as the "Chinzei Nikkō". The Rōmon (tower gate) was modeled after the Yōmeimon of [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] by the restoration craftsmen of the shrine. Along with [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] and [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], Yūtoku Inari Shrine is considered one of the three great Inari shrines in Japan. It attracts 3 million visitors annually and is revered as a guardian deity of all aspects of daily life, including prosperity in business and household fortune. It is also sometimes counted among the four, five, six great Inari shrines of Japan, and the three great Inari shrines of Kyushu. == History == The shrine was established by the first lord of the [[Kashima Domain]], [[Nabeshima Naomasa]], who married Manhime (萬子媛), the daughter of [[Kazan'in Sadamune]], a high-ranking court official. Manhime brought with her a sacred mirror of the Inari deity from the [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto. In Jōkyō 4 (1687), a shrine building was constructed on Mt. Sekkizan, and Manhime herself served the shrine. In Hōei 2 (1705), she performed a ritual fasting until death at the shrine's Sekkizan Cave. Since then, the shrine has been known as "Yūtoku-in" in her honor, and both the Inari deity and Manhime's spirit have been worshipped. With the Meiji era separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the shrine was renamed Yūtoku Inari Shrine. The cave where Manhime entered the state of fasting was designated as the subsidiary Sekkizan Shrine, and she was deified as Mankohime-no-Ōkami. The current main shrine was rebuilt after a fire in 1949, designed by Takashi Kaku, the chief architect of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], and completed in 1957. This is the third reconstruction, with the main buildings lacquered entirely in vermilion. == Grounds == On the west side of the Kinpaku River, the main shrine, Kagura Hall, and Rōmon (tower gate) are located. To the north of the Rōmon is the Sanjusendo Hall, and further north is a Japanese garden. The main shrine is located at a high elevation, accessible by a paid elevator. The approach to the shrine, from the entrance to the shrine grounds to the Yūtoku Bus Terminal (Yūtoku Shrine Mae bus stop), is lined with shops. At the northern end, there is a free parking lot that can accommodate 3,000 cars. Beside the parking lot on the east side of the Kinpaku River, the Yūtoku Museum houses the shrine’s collection of art and local crafts, including armor, swords, and Kashima Nishiki textiles.
